events.title: MNLA Launches Tuareg Rebellion — Mali War Begins
events.detail: The National Movement for the Liberation of Azawad (MNLA), formed largely from Tuareg veterans returning from Libya after Gaddafi's fall, launches simultaneous attacks on Malian army garrisons at Ménaka, Aguelhok, and Tessalit. Within two weeks rebel forces overrun multiple towns across northern Mali. The MNLA's forces include experienced fighters who brought sophisticated weapons from Gaddafi's arsenals.

events.title: MNLA and Ansar Dine Execute ~82 Malian Soldiers at Aguelhok
events.detail: After Malian troops at Aguelhok run out of ammunition and surrender, MNLA and Ansar Dine fighters summarily execute approximately 82 prisoners. Video evidence later documents the killings. The massacre deepens outrage in southern Mali at the government's failure to supply and reinforce its northern garrisons, accelerating the political crisis that leads to the March coup.

events.title: Military Junta Overthrows President Touré — Political Vacuum Opens
events.detail: Captain Amadou Sanogo leads a military mutiny at the Kati garrison, citing the government's failure to supply troops in the north. President Amadou Toumani Touré (ATT) flees the presidential palace and eventually goes into exile in Senegal. ECOWAS immediately suspends Mali. The coup creates a political vacuum that jihadist groups and the MNLA exploit within days to accelerate their northern offensive.

events.title: Rebels Sweep Northern Mali — Kidal, Gao, and Timbuktu Fall Within Days
events.detail: Exploiting the coup's chaos, MNLA and Islamist allies capture Kidal (March 30), Gao (March 31 — with MUJAO involvement), and Timbuktu (early April). Malian military units collapse or retreat south without effective resistance. Within two weeks of the coup, rebels control approximately half of Mali's territory — an area larger than France.

events.title: MNLA Declares Independence of Azawad — Rejected Internationally
events.detail: The MNLA formally declares independence of the State of Azawad, covering the vast northern regions of Kidal, Gao, and Timbuktu. No state recognizes the declaration; the AU, ECOWAS, and UN immediately reject it. The MNLA's secular nationalist project quickly unravels as Islamist allies — Ansar Dine and MUJAO — turn against them in the coming months.

events.title: MUJAO and Ansar Dine Expel MNLA — Islamists Take Full Control of North
events.detail: After a brief failed alliance, MUJAO and Ansar Dine attack the MNLA across all major northern cities. By mid-July the secular nationalist MNLA has been expelled from Gao, Kidal, and Timbuktu. Islamist groups impose Sharia law, close music venues, enforce gender segregation, and begin destroying Sufi religious shrines. The north's governance is now entirely in jihadist hands.

events.title: Ansar Dine Destroys UNESCO-Listed Sufi Shrines in Timbuktu
events.detail: Ansar Dine militants systematically destroy the UNESCO World Heritage-listed mausoleums of Islamic saints in Timbuktu, which they regard as idolatrous. The destruction of Sidi Yahia mosque and multiple shrines shocks the international community. The ICC later prosecutes Ahmad al-Faqi al-Mahdi for the destruction; he pleads guilty in 2016 in the first ICC case involving cultural heritage destruction.

events.title: UN Resolution 2085 Authorizes AFISMA Deployment to Mali
events.detail: UN Security Council Resolution 2085 unanimously authorizes deployment of the African-led International Support Mission in Mali (AFISMA), paving the way for international military intervention. The resolution endorses an ECOWAS plan to deploy up to 3,300 troops and sets conditions on Mali committing to a political transition roadmap. AFISMA is intended to support Malian forces in recovering the north.

events.title: Jihadists Capture Konna — Triggering French Emergency Intervention
events.detail: AQIM, Ansar Dine, and MUJAO forces capture the strategic town of Konna, placing them within 70 km of Sévaré airport — the only military-capable airfield outside Bamako. Amadou Koufa personally leads the assault, briefly declaring himself 'Sultan of Konna.' Malian interim President Traoré declares a state of emergency and requests French military assistance the same day.

events.title: France Launches Operation Serval — Emergency Airstrikes Begin
events.detail: President François Hollande authorizes Operation Serval within hours of Mali's request. French Mirage and Rafale jets begin immediate airstrikes across rebel-held territory from Gao to Léré, while ground troops are airlifted to Bamako. The operation ultimately deploys 4,500 troops, kills approximately 625 militants, and suffers only 7 French fatalities. It becomes France's most rapid decisive military action in Africa since independence.

events.title: AQIM Splinter Seizes BP Gas Facility in Algeria — 38 Foreign Workers Killed
events.detail: In direct retaliation for France's Mali intervention, an AQIM-linked group led by Mokhtar Belmokhtar seizes the Tigantourine gas facility near In Aménas, Algeria, taking hundreds of workers hostage. Algerian special forces storm the facility January 17–19; 38 foreign workers from 10 countries and 29 militants are killed. The crisis demonstrates the regional spillover of the Mali conflict into Algeria.

events.title: French Forces Retake Gao and Timbuktu — Northern Liberation Accelerates
events.detail: Gao is retaken January 25–26 via a special forces night airborne landing. Timbuktu falls January 27 with no resistance — jihadists fled devastating airstrikes. Timbuktu residents welcome French troops with celebrations after nine months of harsh Sharia enforcement. The rapid liberation of these two cities marks the decisive conventional phase of Operation Serval.

events.title: French Forces Secure Kidal — Full Northern Liberation Complete
events.detail: Thirty French special forces commandos, reinforced by Chadian troops, secure Kidal on January 30. Tessalit follows shortly after. By February 8 the conventional phase of Operation Serval is effectively complete; jihadist forces have dispersed into the Sahara or across borders. French commanders shift focus to counter-insurgency and the systematic hunt for jihadist leadership.

events.title: UN Security Council Establishes MINUSMA Peacekeeping Mission
events.detail: UNSC Resolution 2100 unanimously establishes MINUSMA (UN Multidimensional Integrated Stabilization Mission in Mali), authorized at 11,200 military and 1,440 police personnel, effective July 1, 2013. MINUSMA absorbs AFISMA forces and is tasked with supporting Mali's political transition, protecting civilians, and facilitating humanitarian access. It eventually becomes the deadliest UN peacekeeping mission in history.

events.title: IBK Elected President — Civilian Government Restored After Coup
events.detail: Ibrahim Boubacar Keïta (IBK) wins Mali's presidential election with 77.6% in the second round, restoring civilian government after 18 months of junta rule. International observers praise the election as credible despite insecurity in the north. IBK's presidency (2013–2020) is later marked by failure to implement the Algiers Accord, mounting corruption scandals, and exponentially worsening jihadist violence.

events.title: France Launches Operation Barkhane — Permanent Sahel Counter-Terrorism Mission
events.detail: Operation Barkhane officially launches, replacing Operation Serval. At its peak, Barkhane fields 5,500 French troops across Mali, Burkina Faso, Niger, Chad, and Mauritania, with HQ in N'Djamena and major bases in Gao and Niamey. It becomes the longest and most expensive French military operation since World War II, eventually costing over €1 billion annually.

events.title: Amadou Koufa Founds Katiba Macina — Fulani Jihad in Central Mali
events.detail: Amadou Koufa establishes Katiba Macina (Macina Liberation Front) in central Mali's Mopti Region, explicitly targeting the historically marginalized Fulani pastoral communities. The group exploits Fulani grievances over livestock theft, land disputes, and state abuse, framing jihad through a lens of social justice. By 2018, Katiba Macina is linked to 63% of all violent events in central Mali.

events.title: Algiers Accord Signed — Peace Deal Between Mali and Tuareg Groups
events.detail: The Algiers Accord is signed between the Malian government and the Coordination of Azawad Movements (CMA), a Tuareg coalition including the MNLA. The accord envisions decentralization, a development corridor, and integration of Tuareg fighters into the Malian army. Islamist groups (JNIM, AQIM) are excluded. The accord is never meaningfully implemented; both sides exchange accusations of violations throughout the following decade.

events.title: AQIM and al-Mourabitoun Attack Radisson Blu Hotel in Bamako — 22 Killed
events.detail: Gunmen from AQIM and al-Mourabitoun storm the Radisson Blu hotel in central Bamako during a 9-hour siege, killing 22 people including diplomats, UN officials, and foreign businesspeople. Malian special forces supported by French and US special operations personnel conduct the rescue. The attack demonstrates jihadist reach into the capital despite the massive international military presence.

events.title: AQIM Attack on Splendid Hotel in Ouagadougou — 30 Killed
events.detail: Al-Mourabitoun and AQIM gunmen attack the Splendid Hotel and Cappuccino café in central Ouagadougou, killing 30 people including many foreigners. It is Burkina Faso's deadliest terrorist attack to that point, signaling the southward expansion of Sahel jihadism beyond Mali's borders. The attack precedes JNIM's formal founding by 14 months.

events.title: G5 Sahel Political-Security Framework Founded by Five Sahel States
events.detail: Mali, Mauritania, Burkina Faso, Niger, and Chad officially establish the G5 Sahel regional bloc in Nouakchott, Mauritania. Initially a political-economic cooperation framework, the G5 Sahel becomes a security platform in 2017 with the activation of its Joint Force. France and the EU back the group as their preferred framework for Sahelian counter-terrorism regionalization.

events.title: JNIM Founded — Al-Qaeda Unifies Sahel Jihadist Groups
events.detail: Iyad Ag Ghaly publicly announces the formation of Jama'at Nusrat al-Islam wal-Muslimin (JNIM), unifying Ansar Dine, Katiba Macina, al-Mourabitoun, and AQIM's Sahara Emirate under a single al-Qaeda banner. The consolidation creates the most powerful jihadist organization in sub-Saharan Africa. JNIM pledges allegiance to al-Qaeda leader Ayman al-Zawahiri and Afghanistan's Taliban.

events.title: G5 Sahel Joint Force Launches — Five-Nation Counter-Terrorism Command
events.detail: The G5 Sahel Joint Force (FC-G5S) is formally operationalized at Sévaré, Mali, combining troops from Mali, Burkina Faso, Niger, Chad, and Mauritania. Authorized at 5,000 troops covering the tri-border zones, the force is chronically underfunded and repeatedly attacked. The EU pledges €50 million. The force is ultimately destroyed by funding shortfalls, the AES coups, and the withdrawal of three member states.

events.title: Islamic State Officially Recognizes ISGS as Sahel Province Affiliate
events.detail: The Islamic State formally recognizes Adnan Abu Walid al-Sahrawi's group — Islamic State in the Greater Sahara (ISGS) — as an IS affiliate. Al-Sahrawi had pledged allegiance to ISIS in May 2015 after splitting from MUJAO. ISGS focuses on the Liptako-Gourma tri-border zone, distinguishing itself from JNIM through indiscriminate mass violence against civilians of all ethnicities.

events.title: ISGS Ambush Kills 4 US Special Forces and 4 Nigerien Soldiers at Tongo Tongo
events.detail: An ISGS force of 50+ fighters ambushes a 12-man US Special Forces patrol and Nigerien troops near Tongo Tongo in Niger's Tillabéri Region. Four US soldiers (Sgt. La David Johnson and three others) are killed — the largest single US combat loss in Africa since Somalia in 1993. The ambush triggers a congressional investigation into the expanding US military footprint in West Africa.

events.title: Dogon Militia Kill ~160 Fulani Civilians in Ogossagou — Worst Atrocity Since 2012
events.detail: Dan Nan Ambassagou (Dogon hunters' militia) attacks the Fulani village of Ogossagou in Mopti Region, killing approximately 160 civilians — the worst inter-communal violence in Mali's modern history. The attack is a reprisal for cattle raids and jihadist infiltration attributed to Fulani. It dramatically accelerates Fulani radicalization and JNIM recruitment in central Mali.

events.title: ISGS Kills 71 Nigerien Soldiers at Inates — Deadliest Attack in Niger's History
events.detail: ISGS fighters mount a pre-dawn motorcycle assault on the Inates military camp in Niger's Tillabéri Region, killing 71 soldiers in the deadliest attack in Nigerien military history. The attackers breach the perimeter, execute soldiers in their barracks, and seize weapons. President Issoufou declares three days of national mourning and calls an emergency ECOWAS summit.

events.title: Burkina Faso Becomes Epicenter of Sahel Violence
events.detail: Burkina Faso records a dramatic surge in jihadist violence in 2019, with JNIM and ISGS launching hundreds of attacks across the North and East regions. Displacement rises from 47,000 (2018) to 487,000 by year's end. Schools begin closing en masse; border regions slip outside government control. ACLED records 700+ political violence events in 2019 versus 150 in 2016.

events.title: JNIM Attacks G5 Sahel Joint Force Headquarters at Sévaré
events.detail: JNIM launches a complex attack including a car bomb and armed assault on the G5 Sahel Joint Force headquarters at Sévaré, Mali, killing eight and wounding four. The operation is a deliberate strategic strike on the symbol of the Western-backed regional counter-terrorism framework and signals JNIM's ability to strike high-profile international security targets.

events.title: JNIM Attacks French Embassy and Burkinabè Military HQ in Ouagadougou
events.detail: JNIM launches coordinated attacks on the French Embassy and the Burkinabè Army General Staff headquarters in Ouagadougou on the first anniversary of JNIM's founding. Eight security personnel are killed. The attacks demonstrate JNIM's ability to strike the heart of Ouagadougou and signal the group's pan-Sahel ambitions beyond Mali.

events.title: France Claims Koufa Killed — He Reappears Alive Three Months Later
events.detail: French and Malian forces announce Amadou Koufa has been killed in a counter-terrorism operation in central Mali. Koufa reappears in a video message from JNIM media on February 28, 2019, showing him alive and delivering a sermon, embarrassing both governments. The incident highlights the limitations of intelligence gathering in JNIM's decentralized, rural operating environment.

events.title: First Mali Coup — CNSP Ousts President IBK After Mass Protests
events.detail: After months of protests (M5-RFP movement) over corruption, mismanagement, and security failures, the military's National Committee for the Salvation of the People (CNSP), led by Colonel Assimi Goïta, seizes power. IBK is arrested and resigns on television before going into exile. ECOWAS imposes economic sanctions. A transitional civilian government is eventually formed under Bah Ndaw.

events.title: Second Mali Coup — Goïta Arrests Transitional President and PM
events.detail: Vice President Assimi Goïta arrests transitional President Bah Ndaw and PM Moctar Ouane, accusing them of violating the transition charter. On May 28, Mali's constitutional court names Goïta interim president. The 'coup within a coup' terminates the agreed transitional arrangement, fatally damages France-Mali relations, and opens the door for Wagner Group.

events.title: French Drone Kills ISGS Founder Adnan Abu Walid al-Sahrawi
events.detail: A French Reaper drone strikes and kills Adnan Abu Walid al-Sahrawi, founder and leader of ISGS, in the Dangarous Forest near the Mali-Niger border southeast of Gao. He carried a $5 million US bounty. France announces the kill publicly September 16, 2021 after confirmation; the Islamic State confirms in Al-Naba newsletter October 14. He is succeeded by Abu al-Bara al-Sahrawi.

events.title: Wagner Group Mercenaries Deploy to Mali — Replacing French Forces
events.detail: Approximately 1,000 Russian Wagner Group paramilitaries begin arriving in Bamako in December 2021, deploying within weeks to the Mopti region. Fifteen European nations and Canada jointly condemn the deployment. The Malian junta immediately closes its airspace to MINUSMA aviation after Wagner's arrival, blinding the UN mission to Wagner movements and operations.

events.title: First Burkina Faso Coup — Military Ousts President Kaboré
events.detail: Lt. Col. Paul-Henri Sandaogo Damiba leads the MPSR in overthowing President Roch Kaboré, citing the government's failure to contain jihadist violence that had killed thousands and displaced nearly 2 million. Damiba is sworn in February 16. He will himself be ousted 8 months later by an even more hardline officer.

events.title: France Announces Barkhane Withdrawal from Mali After Ambassador Expulsion
events.detail: France announces the suspension of Barkhane in Mali following the junta's expulsion of the French ambassador. Last French Barkhane soldiers cross from Mali into Niger on August 15, 2022. The withdrawal ends the 9-year French counter-insurgency mission in Mali, leaving Wagner Group as Bamako's primary international security partner.

events.title: FAMa and Wagner Forces Kill 500+ Civilians in the Moura Massacre
events.detail: Malian Armed Forces and approximately 100 white soldiers speaking a non-French European language (identified as Wagner mercenaries by witnesses) conduct a 5-day operation in Moura village, executing at least 500 civilians according to the May 2023 UN OHCHR report. Approximately 58 women are subjected to sexual violence. The Malian government claims only 203 'terrorists' were killed. It is the single worst documented atrocity in Mali's decade-long conflict.

events.title: Second Burkina Faso Coup — Captain Traoré Ousts Damiba
events.detail: Captain Ibrahim Traoré, 34, leads a faction that overthrows Damiba, citing his failure to halt jihadist expansion. Traoré becomes the world's youngest head of state, adopting a pan-Africanist, anti-Western posture. He expels French forces in January 2023, invites Wagner support, and dissolves the constitution. Under his rule, government-controlled territory shrinks to an estimated 30–40% of Burkina Faso.

events.title: Mali Withdraws from G5 Sahel — Regional Security Bloc Fractures
events.detail: Mali's junta announces withdrawal from the G5 Sahel and the G5 Joint Force, citing Western political interference and attempts to prevent Mali from chairing the bloc. As a founding member bearing the largest security burden, Mali's withdrawal leaves the G5 Sahel severely weakened and presages the organization's eventual total dissolution in late 2023.

events.title: Burkina Faso Expels French Special Forces — All Three Future AES States Now France-Free
events.detail: The Traoré junta gives France 30 days to withdraw its 400 special forces. French military operations in Burkina Faso formally end February 2023. France's Sahel military presence — once 5,500 troops across five nations — is reduced to Chad and a shrinking Niger contingent. Within 8 months, all three AES junta states have expelled French forces.

events.title: Prigozhin Mutiny in Russia — Wagner Rebranded as Africa Corps
events.detail: Wagner Group founder Yevgeny Prigozhin stages a brief mutiny against Russian military leadership. Although the mutiny is called off within 24 hours, Prigozhin is killed in a plane crash on August 23, 2023. Wagner's Africa operations are subsequently restructured under Russian MoD/GRU direct control and rebranded as 'Africa Corps,' with no operational disruption in Mali, where deployments continue.

events.title: UN Terminates MINUSMA Mandate at Mali Junta's Demand
events.detail: UNSC Resolution 2690 unanimously terminates MINUSMA's mandate with a December 31, 2023 withdrawal deadline, just over 10 years after establishment. Mali's junta had formally demanded termination on June 16, accusing MINUSMA of failing to improve security and sharing intelligence with France. Russia and China use their Security Council roles to limit accountability mechanisms.

events.title: Niger Coup — Presidential Guard General Tchiani Overthrows President Bazoum
events.detail: General Abdourahamane Tchiani, commander of the Presidential Guard, detains President Mohamed Bazoum and proclaims himself CNSP leader on state television. ECOWAS threatens military intervention with an August 6 deadline. Mali and Burkina Faso immediately declare any intervention would be an act of war against them, blocking ECOWAS action and cementing the three-nation anti-Western bloc.

events.title: Mali, Burkina Faso, Niger Sign Alliance of Sahel States Charter
events.detail: Foreign ministers of Mali, Burkina Faso, and Niger sign the AES Charter in Bamako, creating a mutual defense pact modeled on NATO's Article 5. The charter signals the three nations' collective break from ECOWAS and the Western security architecture, replacing it with a Russia-backed regional bloc and announcing plans for a common currency and unified passport.

events.title: FAMa-Wagner Column Departs Gao for Kidal — Largest Offensive in Years
events.detail: A column of ~115 vehicles carrying 600 Malian soldiers and 150–200 Wagner/Africa Corps personnel departs Gao heading north. MINUSMA evacuates its Kidal camp by October 31 under junta orders. The CSP-PSD (Tuareg rebel coalition) briefly occupies the vacated MINUSMA camp. The campaign is the largest Malian military offensive in years and the key test of Wagner's conventional warfare value.

events.title: Wagner/FAMa Capture Kidal — Wagner Hoists Skull Flag Over Historic Fort
events.detail: After fighting November 10–14, a decisive Wagner-led night assault captures Kidal from the Tuareg CSP-PSD coalition. Wagner mercenaries hoist their skull-and-crossbones flag over Kidal Fort on November 22 — a widely circulated image — before the Malian flag replaces it November 26. Critics note JNIM rapidly moves into surrounding territory vacated by the retreating Tuareg forces.

events.title: G5 Sahel Effectively Dissolved — Western-Backed Security Architecture Collapses
events.detail: Following Mali (2022), Burkina Faso and Niger's withdrawals in December 2023, Chad and Mauritania invoke the G5 Sahel charter's dissolution article. The G5 Sahel, established in 2014 as a core Western-backed regional security framework, ceases to function entirely — a symbol of the collapse of the post-2013 counter-terrorism architecture across the Sahel.

events.title: MINUSMA Closes — Deadliest UN Peacekeeping Mission in History Ends
events.detail: MINUSMA's final flag is lowered in Bamako on December 31, 2023, ending the UN mission 10.5 years after its founding. Total deaths: 304 (175 malicious act) — the deadliest UN mission ever. Annual cost: $1.2 billion — the most expensive. Its departure leaves a security vacuum that JNIM rapidly exploits to expand control across central and northern Mali.

events.title: Mali, Burkina Faso, Niger Formally Announce ECOWAS Withdrawal
events.detail: The three AES states jointly announce formal withdrawal from ECOWAS, effective January 2025. The exit severs the trio from ECOWAS's free movement zone, common tariff, and financial safety nets. ECOWAS opens mediation channels but acknowledges limited leverage. The withdrawal deepens the AES's collective alignment with Russia, China, and an explicitly anti-Western regional bloc identity.

events.title: Niger Expels US Forces — America Loses Its Most Critical Sahel Counter-Terrorism Platform
events.detail: Niger's junta demands US withdrawal of approximately 1,000 troops and drones from Air Base 101 (Niamey) and the $110M Air Base 201 (Agadez). The final US withdrawal is completed August 2024, ending America's largest Sahel counter-terrorism platform — including Reaper drones that had tracked ISGS and JNIM leadership for years. Niger requests Russian and Iranian support as replacements.

events.title: Burkinabè Forces Kill 223 Civilians Including 56 Children — Nondin and Soro
events.detail: Burkina Faso's military forces, reportedly supported by VDP (Volunteer Defenders of the Fatherland) militias, summarily execute at least 223 civilians including 56 children in the villages of Nondin and Soro in an apparent reprisal operation. HRW and Amnesty International call the killings potential crimes against humanity. The Burkinabè junta denies the incident and restricts media access.

events.title: Confederation of Sahel States Formally Established
events.detail: The Confederation of Sahel States (CES/AES) is formally inaugurated, upgrading the AES from an alliance to a confederation with planned shared institutions, common currency, joint military command, and unified passport zone. Russia pledges continued arms supply; China signals infrastructure investment interest. The CES positions itself as an explicitly post-Western African governance model.

events.title: JNIM Strikes Kati Military Garrison 15 km from Bamako — First Attack Near Capital
events.detail: JNIM attacks Mali's largest military garrison at Kati using a vehicle-borne IED and small-arms assault — the first direct attack on the greater Bamako area in the conflict's history. Wagner/Africa Corps vehicles are among those destroyed. The attack demonstrates JNIM's expanding operational reach and forces the Goïta junta to acknowledge the capital's security perimeter is now vulnerable.

events.title: EU Takuba Task Force Launched — European Special Forces Deployed to Sahel
events.detail: France establishes the European Task Force Takuba, deploying special forces from 14 European nations to provide direct combat support and mentoring to Malian forces alongside Barkhane. At its peak, Takuba fields ~900 personnel from Sweden, Czech Republic, Denmark, Netherlands, and others. The task force is suspended in March 2022 after Mali expels Swedish forces, and formally dissolved in May 2022.

events.title: JNIM Becomes One of World's Deadliest Terrorist Groups — 46% Surge
events.detail: The Institute for Economics and Peace's Global Terrorism Index 2025 reports JNIM kills 1,454 people in 2024 — a 46% increase over 2023 — making it one of the four deadliest terrorist organizations globally. JNIM now operates across Mali, Burkina Faso, Niger, Senegal, Côte d'Ivoire, Benin, and Togo border areas, averaging 10 deaths per attack.

sources.name: Institute for Economics and Peace — GTI 2025

events.title: JNIM Destroys Boulkessi Base — 60 Killed, 220 Taken Hostage
events.detail: JNIM assaults the Boulkessi military base near the Mali-Burkina Faso border, killing at least 60 of the 280 stationed FAMa soldiers and taking the remainder hostage. The attackers seize 174 Kalashnikovs, 3 vehicles, and communications equipment. JNIM releases a video claiming the operation. It is among the heaviest single-day Malian military losses of the entire war.

events.title: JNIM Strikes Timbuktu — Including Russian Africa Corps Base — 60+ FAMa Killed
events.detail: JNIM launches simultaneous assaults on FAMa positions in Timbuktu: a main military base, three checkpoints, and the Timbuktu airbase hosting Russian Africa Corps personnel. At least 60 FAMa soldiers are confirmed killed. The strike on the Russian base represents a direct challenge to Africa Corps and a significant escalation in JNIM's anti-Russian targeting.

events.title: AES Inaugurates Unified Military Force in Bamako
events.detail: The Alliance of Sahel States inaugurates its Unified Military Force at a Bamako air base, integrating air, land, and intelligence assets from Mali, Burkina Faso, and Niger. The force is backed by Russian arms agreements finalized April 2025. AES leaders present it as the foundation of a post-Western African security architecture — an explicit alternative to NATO-aligned counter-terrorism frameworks.

events.title: Burkina Faso Junta Dissolves All Political Parties — Full Authoritarian Turn
events.detail: Ibrahim Traoré's junta dissolves all political parties in Burkina Faso, formalizing the slide toward one-party military rule. The US expands travel bans on AES states effective January 1, 2026; AES nations impose reciprocal restrictions on US citizens. The dissolution comes as jihadist violence continues at record levels and FAMa-controlled territory remains at an estimated 30–40% of the country.
